entry.title: What a Document AI Workshop Taught Me About Clean Core
entry.content:
There is something I enjoy almost as much as writing content about SAP, and that is reading what people have to say in popular discussion forums regarding topics that may be seen as rather controversial. I think that many times, the fact that some themes generate so much discussion has more to do with people not really understanding them than with the proper complexity of the product, strategy or idea.

In the last couple of weeks, I read a lot of discussion around Clean Core, both in SAP Community and in these online forums. I was amazed by how people defined the concept. I mean, you could tell that the opinion they were communicating was, of course, directly linked to the way they each define Clean Core, and that is exactly what surprised me: everyone defined it differently. Some described Clean Core as a way to reduce customization, others associated it with cloud ERP, and a few questioned whether it was even realistic in large SAP environments. 

This reading coincided with the preparation and hosting of our hands-on SAP Document AI workshop together with SAP, although at first I didn’t see any connection between the discussions about Clean Core and the hackathon, by the end of the workshop, I found myself thinking about Clean Core in a way I hadn't before. 

In this case, our workshop was focused on a particular scenario involving document processing. Prior to the event, we had already created a Proof of Concept for one of our customers, who was working with a huge number of documents related to their operations from various regions and business units. The problem lay in the fact that it was not just about data extraction from invoices and tickets. Rather, the issue here was the high percentage of manual labor required for proper document classification, validation, and linking every file to the right business process. During our preparation for the presentation, we paid most of our attention to AI possibilities provided by SAP Document AI.

The unexpected aspect of the workshop discussions turned out to be the shift of focus to an area completely unrelated to what was being discussed in the first place. Naturally, people did show interest in the accuracy of document extraction, and in capabilities of AI as well. However, most of the questions were related to integration and scalability, how the solution could work in participants' environments, how well it would integrate with other applications, and how it would help them expand their capabilities without disrupting existing processes. 

Looking back, this is probably where the connection with Clean Core started to become clearer for me. Throughout the workshop, nobody was asking how to modify SAP so that Document AI could work. Nobody was discussing new custom tables, custom transactions, or large changes to core ERP processes. Several participants were already thinking about additional use cases, while others wanted to understand how the approach could scale across different document types and business units. The most prominent questions were adoption, integration, operational impact, and long-term scalability. The conversations focused on how to introduce a new capability while keeping the existing landscape stable.

That realization made me think about many of the online discussions I had been reading. A large number of them seemed to describe Clean Core primarily in terms of restrictions. The conversation often revolved around what organizations should avoid doing, what should not be customized, or what should be moved outside the ERP. While those aspects are certainly part of the concept, the workshop made me wonder if that perspective is too narrow.

What I saw during the event was not a limitation on innovation. If anything, it was the opposite. SAP Document AI was introducing completely new capabilities into existing business processes. The innovation was real, the business value was clear, and yet none of it required redesigning the ERP core itself. For the first time, I started thinking about Clean Core not as a strategy for reducing customization, but as a strategy for deciding where innovation should happen.

The more I thought about it after the workshop, the more I understood why so many people define Clean Core differently. Some people approach it from an architecture perspective, others from governance, and others from the realities of cloud ERP. Not one of these definitions can be wrong per se. But I found myself asking whether it is because of the lack of time spent on analyzing what the tool actually does for us and allows us to do.

In retrospect, what really got through to me at the workshop was not the demo as such. We had worked on getting ready the Proof of Concept, exercises and scenarios that would be demonstrated at the workshop during weeks in advance. What stayed with me after the workshop were the discussions triggered by all these. The most engaging ones did not concern the changes in ERP but rather organization of the processes that allowed introducing new functionality and retaining flexibility in the process. As much as I am not sure yet that there is such a definition of Clean Core accepted by all, after reading multiple threads online I am only even surer about this.

For me, Clean Core is no longer just about keeping systems standard. It is about creating the conditions for innovation to happen without making future change more difficult than it needs to be. Perhaps that is why I now see Clean Core less as a technical principle and more as a question every organization eventually has to answer: where should new capabilities live?

entry.title: AI Product Management: Autonomy Is Earned, Not Scheduled
entry.content:
AI Product Management series

Here is a sentence that appears, in some form, in most agentic rollout plans: "After thirty days of supervised operation, we will move the agent to full autonomy." Or "after five hundred clean actions." Or "following the first quarterly review."

Every one of those is a schedule. None of them is a safety criterion. A schedule measures how much time has passed or how many actions have accumulated. It does not measure whether the agent has demonstrated, in the failure modes that actually matter for this decision type, that it can be trusted with more authority. Confusing the two is one of the most common and most expensive governance mistakes in agentic AI, and it is worth taking apart.

The autonomy ladder

In deterministic SaaS you were trained to think in terms of feature completeness. A workflow was either automated or it was not. For agents, full automation is not a starting point. It is an earned state, and the climb to it has rungs.

Rung 1 is augmentation. The agent surfaces information and prepares options; the human decides. The PM's job here is the quality of the recommendation and how uncertainty is communicated, so you do not create blind trust disguised as assistance.

Rung 2 is limited automation with approval gates. The agent executes bounded actions after human confirmation. The PM's job is the approval moment: the decision package the human sees, how uncertainty and alternatives appear, and what happens if nobody responds within the defined window.

Rung 3 is semi-autonomous operation with guardrails. The agent acts within a defined scope and escalates outside it. The PM's job is the escalation triggers, the audit surface, the recovery workflow, and, critically, the evidence required before the system earns this level of authority.

The rungs are not a timeline. They are a sequence gated by demonstrated competence. Movement up requires demonstrated performance at the rung below, documented evidence of safe operation, and explicit criteria for what "safe enough" means for this specific decision type.

What scheduling actually measures

Most teams in practice schedule the climb. They set a date or a threshold count, and when it arrives, the agent moves up. This feels rigorous because it is quantified. A number is involved. A milestone is hit.

But look at what the number measures. Thirty days measures the passage of time. Five hundred clean actions measures volume of activity. Neither measures whether the agent has been tested against the cases that determine whether it is safe at the next rung. An agent can run five hundred clean refunds and never once encounter the fraud-pattern case that is the entire reason a human boundary exists. Scheduling promotes it anyway, on day thirty-one, into authority over exactly the cases it has never been shown to handle.

The clean record is not evidence of competence on the hard cases. It is usually evidence that the hard cases have not come up yet. Time in production is not the same as coverage of the failure surface. A schedule conflates them, and that conflation is where the incident comes from.

This is governance theater: the appearance of a careful, criteria-based promotion process, built on a criterion that does not measure the thing it is supposed to gate.

What earned promotion requires instead

Earned autonomy replaces the calendar with evidence. To move an agent up a rung, you require three things, defined before launch, not negotiated after the first incident.

First, demonstrated performance at the current rung, measured against the failure modes that matter for this decision type, not against aggregate volume. The question is not "how many actions has it taken?" but "has it been tested, repeatedly, on the specific cases where being wrong is expensive, and did it hold?" This is where your eval set earns its keep: it is the curated collection of hard cases, including the never-ship failures, that the agent must clear before promotion is even on the table.

Second, documented evidence of safe operation, meaning the audit surface actually produced the instruments that let you see how the agent behaved, not just that it completed tasks. The gap between task completion and genuine success, the unintended-action rate, the override pattern on escalations: these are what tell you whether the clean record reflects competence or absence of hard cases.

Third, an explicit, written definition of "safe enough" for the next rung. Safe enough is not universal. The threshold for promoting a support-ticket categorizer is not the threshold for promoting an agent with payment authority. Write the threshold down before launch, because a threshold defined after the first incident is defined under pressure, by people who want the problem to go away.

The piece nobody writes down: demotion triggers

There is a direction on the ladder that rollout plans almost never address. They specify the climb. They never specify the fall.

Before launch, define the demotion triggers: the thresholds on unintended actions, override frequency, or incident severity that force the agent back down a rung. What rate of wrong autonomous actions pulls it from rung 3 to rung 2? What override pattern says the humans no longer trust it and the boundary needs to tighten? What single incident is severe enough to demote on its own?

If demotion criteria are not defined before launch, they will be defined under pressure after the first incident, which is the worst possible time to be designing a control. In the moment, there is an outage, a frustrated executive, and a team improvising a response to a situation nobody planned for. The demotion trigger written calmly in advance is a control. The one improvised during an incident is damage control.

An agent that can only climb is not on a ladder. It is on an escalator with no stop button.

Three decisions, one discipline

Earned autonomy is not a single gate. It is the same discipline applied at three points in the lifecycle.

Pre-build, you decide whether the problem is suitable for an agent at all. Pre-launch, you decide whether the agent has earned its starting rung, the level of authority it ships with on day one, which for a consequential decision type is rarely the top of the ladder. Post-launch, you decide, repeatedly, whether it has earned the next one, on evidence, against criteria you wrote before you had any reason to want a particular answer.

Three decisions, one discipline: authority follows demonstrated competence, in the failure modes that matter, measured against criteria set in advance. Not a date. Not a count of clean runs. Evidence that the agent can be trusted with the specific cases the next rung exposes it to.

The calendar is comfortable because it is automatic. It promotes the agent without anyone having to make a judgment call. That is exactly the problem. The judgment call is the job. An agent's authority over consequential decisions is precisely the thing that should never be on autopilot, and a schedule is autopilot wearing the costume of a process.

entry.title: AI Product Management: The Two Documents That Replace the PRD
entry.content:
AI Product Management series

For twenty years the artifact was the PRD. You wrote it, handed it to engineering, watched it get decomposed into an epic, and the team built it. That model worked because the hard part was building. With agentic AI, the hard part is no longer building. A coding agent will produce a working prototype of almost anything you can describe in an afternoon. The hard part is deciding what the agent should do, where its authority stops, and how the people around it will know when it is wrong.

So the artifact splits in two. And the most common way good agent projects go wrong is that the second document gets written and the first never does.

This post is about both documents and the discipline of writing them in the right order.

The decision and the build are different documents

Call them the Human Brief and the Executable Brief.

The Human Brief is the document a room argues with. It is the PRD's successor, written in prose: the business case, the go/no-go, the cost model, where the boundary sits and why. Its audience is the executive sponsor, finance, engineering, design, and the domain expert. Its purpose is to decide whether to build at all, before anyone writes code.

The Executable Brief is the document a system acts on. It is the epic's successor, written as numbered, testable requirements: the behavior, the supervisory experience, and the governance, precise enough to generate a working prototype this week and seed a backlog next. Its audience is the prototype, the coding agent, and the team.

You derive the second from the first. The decision comes before the specification, always, and skipping the Human Brief is how a team ends up six months into building something nobody ever decided was worth building.

What both briefs share

Here is the part that is easy to get wrong. The temptation is to put the agent in one document and the supervision in the other. That is exactly how the supervision ends up unbuilt.

Both briefs carry both channels: Channel 1, the agent and what it does, and Channel 2, the system that supervises it. The split between the two briefs is by audience and purpose, decide versus build, not by channel.

In the Human Brief, the supervisory layer appears as a decision the room owns: where the boundary sits, what it costs to fund the oversight honestly, who is accountable for a wrong outcome. In the Executable Brief, the same supervisory layer appears as buildable requirements: the autonomy boundary, the approval experience, the audit surface, the eval set. The supervision gets built because it was written into the spec as a requirement, not because someone remembered it at the end.

The Human Brief: the document you argue with

A Human Brief contains, in prose a room can debate:

The problem and the opportunity. What is costing money or going wrong today, in one honest paragraph, including the size.

What you are building, and what you are deliberately not. The intended behavior in plain terms, and the adjacent thing it must not become, which is usually the failure mode of the obvious version. "Resolve refunds automatically" must not quietly become "approve refunds the company should have questioned."

The cases that decide whether it is worth building. The hard cases the agent must handle well, not the easy ones that were never the problem. The in-window defective item was always going to be refunded; the agent earns nothing there. It earns its place on the request just outside the window where the customer is right on the merits, the high-value account near churn, the amount ten times the others on an account that smells like fraud.

The business case and the go/no-go. The suitability tests, the cost model with real numbers, and an explicit decision gate. This is the section a finance partner will press on, and the place where the trap lives: the model's token cost per decision is a few cents, far below a human's time, and that is not the real cost. The real cost is the architecture multiplier, orchestration, integrations, the audit pipeline, eval maintenance, plus the human reviewing every escalated case. The break-even is not "agent cheaper than human per task." It is "fully loaded cost, including supervising the escalated minority, below the cost of humans reviewing all of it, with the wrongful-action risk priced in." If the only way the math works is by under-funding the human review, the answer is no.

Where the boundary sits, and why. The autonomy limit and the escalation triggers, stated as a deliberate choice with its risk named. This is the Channel 2 decision the room owns. You are choosing, explicitly, which kind of wrong is tolerable and which is never tolerable even once. That asymmetry is what the room should argue about.

Accountability and how you will know. Who owns a wrong outcome, named before launch, not in the incident review by pointing at whoever is nearest. And the instruments that will signal drift before the loss shows up.

What success looks like, and what it does not. The real definition of done, written to exclude the seductive wrong metric, which for most agents is "share resolved without a human." An agent that resolves everything has almost certainly approved things it should have questioned.

The open questions the room still owes. The business decisions not yet made, each flagged in the open. A question named here is one the room knows it must answer. A question left out is one that gets answered by default, in code, by whoever ships first.

The Executable Brief: the document a system acts on

The Executable Brief takes three disciplines from spec-driven development tooling (GitHub's Spec Kit is the clearest current instance) and adds the part those tools do not give you.

Requirements are numbered and written as testable statements, not prose, so each can be checked off rather than interpreted. Every place the spec is silent gets an explicit clarification marker rather than a quiet assumption, because that marker is the difference between a gap you decided to leave and a gap the coding agent fills for you with whatever the simplest path suggests. And the behavior is graded against acceptance scenarios in given/when/then form a machine can run.

The structure is borrowed. The supervisory content, the Channel 2 requirements, is the part this brief exists to add.

It contains the system type (suggestion engine, copilot, or autonomous actor, because each carries a different accountability model); an outcome spec stated as a target over a distribution of cases, not a pass/fail story; the behavior as numbered functional requirements (FR-1, FR-2 ...); acceptance scenarios including the hard ones; the supervisory experience, what the human sees and does at the approval moment; the governance as numbered requirements (GR-1, GR-2 ...) covering the autonomy boundary, audit surface, and recovery workflow; measured success criteria stated as numbers over the distribution; the eval set with the endorsed outcome for each case; and the gate the spec must pass before it proceeds.

One functional requirement is worth seeing, because it is the whole point of the exercise. For a refund agent: When the customer's stated reason conflicts with the order record, the resolution is unresolved and flagged for clarification: trust the record, trust the customer, or escalate. The coding agent must not pick for us. That conflict is a real decision with real exposure. Leaving it unmarked is how it gets decided silently, in code, by whoever takes the simplest path.

And one governance requirement that names the non-negotiable: The boundary must be enforced in the execution path before the action fires, not logged after. A boundary that lives in the prompt is a suggestion. A boundary in the payment path is a control.

Why not just write a user story?

A working PM will object that this is a lot of apparatus for what used to be a user story and an acceptance criterion. The comparison is the whole argument.

The old way writes the refund behavior as a story. As a customer, when I request a refund for a defective item, the agent issues the refund, so that I am made whole without waiting. It is clean. It would pass grooming in any backlog in the world. And it is the wrong primitive, because it describes the case that was never hard. What the story cannot say is what the agent should do with the request just outside the window, or the churning customer worth more than the refund, or the amount that smells like fraud. Those are the cases the agent is actually judged on, and the story has no field for any of them.

The reason is structural. The user story works because ordinary software is deterministic: one correct output, and a test that runs once and passes is a real answer. None of that holds for an agent's judgment. The output is a distribution. "Correct" is a judgment, not a binary. A test that passes once tells you little, because the next run may differ.

So the work-unit for the agent's behavior shifts from the user story to an outcome-centric spec: the outcome you want, the bounds the agent must stay inside, and the eval set that grades whether it got there, including what acceptable failure looks like. A deterministic spec says do X then Y. An outcome-centric spec says achieve this outcome, within these bounds, and here is how we will know.

The user story is not dead. The deterministic shell around the agent, the orchestration, the integrations, the screens the user clicks, is still ordinary software and still gets ordinary stories. What resists the story is the probabilistic core, the agent's judgment itself. The signal to watch for: when you find yourself writing an acceptance criterion for an agent's judgment and it reads as pass/fail, that is the deterministic tool on the probabilistic core, and the case you are not writing down is usually the one that reaches the person the agent affects.

The operating model in one breath

Decide in the Human Brief. Specify in the Executable Brief. Build the agent inside the boundary the brief set. Watch it for as long as it runs. Everything downstream, the runtime design, the evals, the production observation, is the brief's requirements made real, and after launch the supervisory track keeps running while the rest winds down.

Two documents, in order. Write the second one without the first, and you have built something well that nobody decided to build.

entry.title: Re: Managing JMS Queues Programmatically with the new SAP Cloud Integration JMS OData API
entry.content:
Really appreciate the detailed breakdown of the JMS OData API. The move and retry operations stand out as a big step toward operational automation.

In real projects, we often had to rely on manual monitoring UI or custom scripts for reprocessing failed messages. Having filter-based move and retry APIs now opens up possibilities for building automated recovery pipelines (especially for correlationId-based reprocessing).

One thing I’m curious about — have you seen customers implementing time-window slicing combined with scheduled jobs to handle high-volume queues reliably?

entry.title: Displaying CDS Views in ALV using SALV IDA
entry.content:
Displaying CDS Views in ALV using SALV IDA

Traditional ALV reports require fetching data into internal tables before displaying it. With SALV IDA (Integrated Data Access), data is read directly from the database, leveraging the power of SAP HANA for optimized performance.

In this example, we consume the CDS View ZI_SO_DETAILS and display it in a full-screen ALV without creating any internal table. This approach is ideal for large datasets and modern SAP S/4HANA developments.

 

 

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report : ZCDS_DISPLAY_IN_ALVFORM
*& Author : Prabhat Mishra
*& Purpose :
*& This report demonstrates how to display a CDS View using
*& ALV with Integrated Data Access (IDA).
*&
*& Key Features:
*& - Direct consumption of CDS Views in ALV
*& - HANA-optimized data retrieval
*& - No internal table required
*& - Built-in sorting, filtering, and aggregation
*& - Full-screen ALV display using SALV IDA framework
*&
*& CDS View Used : ZI_SO_DETAILS
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

REPORT zcds_display_in_alvform.

DATA : lo_alv TYPE REF TO if_salv_gui_table_ida,
lo_display TYPE REF TO if_salv_gui_table_display_opt,
lo_screen TYPE REF TO if_salv_gui_fullscreen_ida.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Create ALV IDA instance for CDS View
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
TRY.
CALL METHOD cl_salv_gui_table_ida=>create_for_cds_view
EXPORTING
iv_cds_view_name = 'ZI_SO_DETAILS'
RECEIVING
ro_alv_gui_table_ida = lo_alv.

CATCH cx_salv_db_connection.
CATCH cx_salv_db_table_not_supported.
CATCH cx_salv_ida_contract_violation.
CATCH cx_salv_function_not_supported.
ENDTRY.

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Configure ALV display settings
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
lo_alv->display_options(
RECEIVING ro_display_options = lo_display ).

* Set report title
lo_display->set_title(
iv_title = 'Manage Sales Order' ).

* Enable zebra pattern for better readability
lo_display->enable_alternating_row_pattern( ).

* Set focus on ALV output
lo_display->set_focus( ).

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* Display CDS data in Full-Screen ALV
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
lo_alv->fullscreen(
RECEIVING ro_fullscreen = lo_screen ).

lo_screen->display( ).

entry.title: Re: Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products
entry.content:
Hi Chitti,

You can achieve this requirement by following the steps below:

  1. Ensure that the same Consolidation Group is assigned across the deliveries that need to be picked together.
  2. Maintain the required settings in the following SPRO path:

SCM EWM → EWM → Mobile Data Entry → Define Combined Picking Profile

First, create and define a Combined Picking Profile (for example, CP1) and select the "Consolidation Group" checkbox.

Once the profile is configured, it can be assigned at the Warehouse Process Type (WPT) level, the Activity Area (AA) level, or both. In my example, the profile is assigned at both levels.

Additionally, the Combined Picking functionality can be used together with 2-Step Picking, as described in SAP Help. This is particularly useful when multiple deliveries (for example, for different customers) require picking from the same storage bin, allowing warehouse operators to optimize picking activities.

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: We want to know where FU_CODE Follow up action code a...
entry.content:
Hi,

The follow-up activity is not stored in database table for an existing returns order.

When you display a returns order, the follow-up activity will be determined by reading chain customizing with Chain ID stored in table MSR_D_EXECUTED.

Method GET_DATA(Class CL_MSR_TRC_DB)

* determine follow-up code
  cl_msr_def_db=>get_chain_data(
    EXPORTING
      iv_chain_id = ls_executed-chain_id
    IMPORTING
      es_chain    = ls_chain ).

  es_comm_ctrl_db-fu_code        = ls_chain-fu_code.

entry.title: Stay Certified, Stay Ahead: New Assessment Questions for C_S4PM
entry.content:
Your SAP Certification is more than a milestone—it’s your proof of expertise. Today, we’re excited to share that a new version of Stay Certified assessment questions is now available for:

SAP Certified – Managing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Projects (C_S4PM)

Keep Your Skills Fresh and Relevant
Earning your certification is just the beginning. To maintain its value, it’s essential to stay aligned with the latest innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

The Stay Certified program helps you:

  • Stay up to date with product and process changes
  • Reinforce real-world project knowledge
  • Maintain a certification that reflects current expertise

What’s New in the Assessment?
The updated Stay Certified assessment includes:

  • Refreshed questions aligned with the latest releases
  • Real-life project scenarios for deeper understanding
  • A focused and efficient way to validate your knowledge

Check Your Deadline – Stay on Track!
To keep your certification valid, you must complete the Stay Certified assessment within 1 year after getting certified.
Follow these steps to check your due date:
learning.sap.com → My Learning → My Certifications → Manage Certification
You will also receive an email reminder 8 weeks before your due date—but why wait? Get ahead and complete it early!

Take Action Today
Staying certified is not just a requirement—it’s a commitment to excellence and continuous learning.
Take a few minutes today to check your status, explore the updated questions, and secure your certification for the future.

Let’s keep learning. Let’s keep growing. Let’s Stay Certified

entry.title: Re: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Interesting - thanks for taking the time to capture this/provoke thinking around this. I have seen SAP business network pitched a lot in all the SAP products e.g SAP CIM, SAP fieldglass and recently also in context of SAP EAM exchanging info between client and OEM suppliers. I see the potential to reduce process friction, enhance collaborations and improve real time transparency.  We typically have smart follower approach though specifically and before even considering the agents scenarios,  really do wonder 

- how mature all of these business network scenarios are already?

- how extensive already used in the wild?

- cost involved outweight the TCO (incl transaction/license cost)?

entry.title: Employee as BP
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently in the Go-Live phase of our project, and the HR module has not yet been implemented. However, the customer has requested that their Sales Employees be created as Business Partners in the production system.

At present, can we create these Sales Employees as Business Partners without assigning the Employee role (BUP003)? If so, when SAP SuccessFactors (or the HR module) is implemented later, will it be possible to link these same Business Partner records to the HR employee master data instead of creating new records?

If this approach is feasible, I would appreciate guidance on the configurations, master data settings, and best practices that should be considered now to ensure a smooth future integration with SuccessFactors/HR. Specifically, I would like to understand any critical settings that should not be missed during the initial Business Partner creation process to avoid issues during employee synchronization and integration at a later stage.

Please share your recommendations and experiences.

Best Regards,

Dinesh

entry.title: Re: Can the reconciliation / payable account be determine...
entry.content:
Good day @byd 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, for one and the same Business Partner (BP), the reconciliation account is not determined automatically by transaction origin, such as SD versus FI.

The reconciliation account is normally taken from the BP company code data. Alternative reconciliation accounts can be used in manual FI posting scenarios only when the reconciliation account field is enabled for input. KBA 2853117 and 2844132 gives good explanation.

So, in simple terms:

  • One BP cannot have automatic payable reconciliation account determination based only on SD origin versus FI origin.
  • Alternative reconciliation accounts are mainly for supported manual FI invoice postings where the field is ready for input.
  • They do not solve automatic SD driven account determination for the same BP.
  • Special G/L is also not a flexible workaround because new special G/L indicators cannot be created or changed in Public Cloud. See KBA 2761212 on the restriction.
  • A substitution or BAdI should not be used to replace the reconciliation account of a supplier or customer subledger line after determination. The reconciliation account is a control account and must remain consistent with subledger accounting.

 

To your questions:

Question 1: For one and the same Business Partner, is it possible to have the reconciliation account determined automatically based on the originating module (SD vs FI)?

Answer: No. There is no standard origin-based reconciliation account determination for the same BP

Question 2: Given the constraints above, is there any standard approach in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to achieve origin-based determination at all?

Answer: Not fully. The standard options are:

  • Use the BP reconciliation account for the normal process.
  • Use alternative reconciliation account only in supported manual FI postings.
  • Use separate BP supplier/customer master data if the accounting treatment must be different and automated.

Question 3: If standard cannot cover it, does it require custom development? Under Public Cloud extensibility (BAdI / key-user / developer extensibility), which option — if any — would apply, or is splitting into two Business Partners the only viable path?

Answer: In Public Cloud, custom development is not the recommended way to change the reconciliation account determination for subledger postings. If the requirement is mandatory and must be automated, the cleanest viable design is to use separate BP or separate supplier/customer account roles representing trade and non-trade liabilities.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Custom CDS Error "Define one key field as a represent...
entry.content:
Good day @yukitsumura 

Thank you for your question.

Maintaining a field in View Properties --> Representative Key is not enough for an Analytical Dimension. The representative key must also follow the analytical modelling rules for dimensions. Note that for a dimension, all key fields except the Representative key field and date or time fields must have an association to a master data view. See KBA 2930441 and note 2657021 for more details.

In other words:

You must mark the representative key as one of the key fields.

There should be only one field selected as Representative key.

Other key fields in the dimension normally need a valid master data association.

If the selected representative key was added or changed after the view was created, the app my not accept the key structure. KBA 2726065 highlights the need of a custom CDS view key and its importance of remaining unchanged after creation. Also see KBA 3357030 for more details.

Here is a recommended correction:

Open the custom CDS view in Custom CDS Views. Go to Elements, check how many fields are marked as key.            

Keep only the true business key as the key, where possible. Go to View Properties --> General, set the same key field as the Representative Key.   

For any other field that must remain, maintain the required master data association. Save the draft, run check and again.

If you still face the error, recreate the custom CDS with the correct key structure from the beginning. This is often faster because existing custom CDS view key cannot always be changed cleanly after they are created. See KBA 2726056 for more details.

To summarise:

  • The error usually appears because the CDS view does not fully meet the analytical dimension modelling rules.
  • The field selected as Representative Key must also be a key field, and other key fields generally need valid master data associations. If the key structure was changed after the custom CDS view was created, recreate the view with the correct key design and then publish it again.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: NFS-e Via – Fiscal Bookkeeping in SAP S/4HANA Cloud P...
entry.content:
Hello @j35400__ 

Based on the generated translation. 

Com base na tradução gerada.

Obrigado pela sua pergunta.

No caso da Nuvem Pública, não vejo um tipo de documento fiscal padrão que seja especificamente identificado como NFS-e Via. A localização padrão brasileira oferece suporte à NFS-e por meio da Conformidade de Documentos e Relatórios, mas a documentação padrão disponível se refere à NFS-e como um documento de serviço eletrônico, e não como um tipo de documento separado especificamente para a NFS-e Via – Nota Fiscal de Serviço de Exploração de Vias.  Consulte a KBA 3599307 para obter mais detalhes.

Em termos simples:

  •     Se o NFS-e Via for emitido por um sistema externo, o SAP não deve ser considerado como o sistema emissor.
  •     É possível usar o SAP para registrar o impacto contábil e tributário, desde que o código de imposto, o código de serviço, o município, o valor base, a alíquota e as referências fiscais estejam corretamente atualizados.
  •     Não tenho conhecimento de um processo padrão especificamente denominado NFS-e Via na Nuvem Pública.
  •     Se sua necessidade for apenas contabilidade fiscal e declaração de impostos, a abordagem prática é registrá-la como um documento fiscal/contábil relacionado a serviços e manter o NFS-e Via emitido externamente como referência ou anexo.
  •     Se a necessidade for a declaração eletrônica ou a transmissão do layout específico do NFS-e Via, isso deve ser validado com o SAP DRC ou com o suporte de localização do SAP Brasil.
  •     Para campos específicos do NFS-e, o SAP oferece opções de extensibilidade, por exemplo, usando BAdI para preencher campos de texto adicionais da Nota Fiscal ou do NFS-e. Consulte o KBA 3325393 para obter mais informações.

A abordagem recomendada é a seguinte:
1. Certifique-se de registrar a nota fiscal de prestação de serviços de fornecedor/cliente no SAP utilizando o processo financeiro relevante.
2. Atualize o código de tributação brasileiro correto e os dados fiscais relacionados ao serviço.
3. Insira o código de serviço 220101 e o código NBS 106042100 nos campos de localização disponíveis.
4. Armazene o XML ou o NFS-e Via emitido externamente como um anexo ou referência externa.
5. Verifique se o relatório fiscal utilizado pelo cliente aceita esse lançamento para a declaração de impostos.
6. Se o documento precisar ser monitorado, transmitido, cancelado ou declarado eletronicamente como um layout específico de NFS-e Via, é necessário confirmar a compatibilidade com o SAP DRC/localização brasileira.

A abordagem recomendada é a seguinte:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SLH_NFe/71b3b178e5cb4a50a88cf2985a14f17c/abf6ebe91b1443fe92b3254d31997f3a.html?locale=en-US

 

 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, I do not see a standard fiscal document type that is specifically separated as NFS-e Via. The Brazilian standard localization supports NFS-e through Document and Reporting Compliance, but the available standard documentation refers to NFS-e as an electronic service document, not as a separate document type specifically for NFS-e Via - Nota Fiscal de Serviço de Exploração de Vias.  See KBA 3599307 for more details.

In simple terms:

  • If the NFS-E Via is issued by an external system, SAP should not be treated as the issuing system.
  • You can use SAP to record the accounting and tax impact, provided the tax code, service code, municipality, base amount, tax rate, and fiscal references are correctly maintained.
  • I am not aware of a standard process specifically named NFS-e Via in Public Cloud.
  • If your requirement is only fiscal accounting and tax reporting, the practical approach is to record it as a service-related fiscal/accounting document and keep the externally issued NFS-e Via as reference or attachment.
  • If the requirement is electronic reporting or transmission of the specific NFS-e Vial layout, this must be validated with SAP DRC or SAP Brazil localisation support.
  • For specific NFS-e fields, SAP provides extensibility options, for example using BAdI to fill additional Nota Fiscal or NFS-e service text fields. See KBA 3325393 for more insights.

 

The recommended approach is as follows:

  1. Ensure you register the supplier/customer service invoice in SAP using the relevant finance process.
  2. Maintain the correct Brazilian tax code and service-related fiscal data.
  3. Capture the service code 220101 and NBS code 106042100 where supported by the available localisation fields.
  4. Store the XML or DANFS-e Via issued externally as an attachment or external reference.
  5. Validate whether the fiscal report used by the customer accepts this posting for tax declaration.
  6. If the document must be electronically monitored, transmitted, cancelled, or reported as a specific NFS-e Via layout, you need to confirm support with SAP DRC/Brazilian localisation.

 

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SLH_NFe/71b3b178e5cb4a50a88cf2985a14f17c/abf6ebe91b1443fe92b3254d31997f3a.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

“SAP GenAI was used to help generate  this content”

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
The RAG Chunking Strategies that can actually survive Production
entry.content:
The Chunking Strategies That Actually Survive Production

If your RAG quality has plateaued, the bottleneck is almost never your vector DB or your reranker. It is almost always your chunker. The naive splitters in every quickstart tutorial destroy the one thing retrieval depends on: context.

This is a tour of the chunking strategies you will actually meet in production. For each one I cover how it works, why you would reach for it, why you would not, and where it fits. I am avoiding hard benchmark numbers on purpose - the field moves fast and figures age badly. What does not age is the mechanism each strategy relies on, and the corpus shape it was built for.

Why Chunking Is The Hidden Lever

A retriever can only return what your indexer produced. If your chunks are mid-sentence fragments stripped of context, no embedder, reranker, or LLM downstream will recover what you destroyed at ingest. Most teams over-invest in retrieval and under-invest in chunking. The fix is upstream.

Two failure modes show up over and over:

  1. The fragment problem. A chunk reading "set the timeout to 30 seconds in the advanced settings tab" is meaningless without knowing what product, what version, and which settings tab.
  2. The boundary problem. A chunk that ends mid-table, mid-code-block, or mid-sentence has lost the structural cue the author wrote precisely to communicate meaning.

Every strategy below is a different answer to those two problems.

Strategy 1: Fixed-Size Chunking

Workflow. Cut the document every N characters or N tokens, optionally with M characters of overlap. No grammar, no structure, no embeddings. Pure counting.

Example. A document about Kubernetes controllers gets sliced every 512 characters. One chunk ends with "the controller watches for chan" and the next begins with "ges and reconciles state". Both chunks are now noise.

Pros.

  • Trivial to implement.
  • Predictable token budget per chunk.
  • Cheapest possible ingest.

Cons.

  • Splits mid-sentence and mid-word.
  • Breaks back-references. A chunk that says "the controller does X" no longer tells you which controller, because the noun it referred to is in a different chunk.
  • Splits tables, code, equations down the middle.
  • Embeddings get noisy because chunks are not self-contained.

When to use. As the baseline you measure improvement against. Acceptable if your corpus is short, uniform, and pre-cleaned (chat transcripts where each turn stands alone, log lines with fixed structure).

When NOT to use. Anywhere else. Treat it as a demo strategy. If a tutorial proposes this for a production RAG, the tutorial is wrong.

Strategy 2: Recursive Character Splitting

Workflow. Try the most semantic separator first (a blank line between paragraphs), fall back to weaker ones (single newline, space, character-level splits) if chunks remain too large. LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter default separator list contains exactly four entries: paragraph break, line break, space, and an empty-string fallback.

How it works. If a paragraph fits within your chunk size, keep it whole. If not, drop down to sentences. If a sentence is still too long, drop to words. The hierarchy of separators is your knob.

Example. A blog post with clean paragraph breaks chunks naturally on the blank lines and you get clean paragraph chunks. A dense PDF dump with no paragraph breaks falls all the way through to space-splitting and you get fragments again.

Pros.

  • Cheap, fast, deterministic.
  • Respects paragraph and sentence boundaries when the document has them.
  • The "no-thought" choice that mostly works as a starting point.

Cons.

  • Document-blind. Does not understand that ## Section Header matters more than a regular newline.
  • Will still split inside tables and code unless you customise separators.
  • Treats every newline as equal weight.

When to use. Your default starting point on a new corpus. Run it before reaching for anything fancier. Strong baseline against which everything else must justify itself.

When NOT to use. As a permanent answer for structured documents (Markdown, HTML, code, layout-rich PDFs). It cannot exploit structure it does not see.

Strategy 3: Sentence-Based Splitting

Workflow. Use a real linguistic tokenizer (NLTK Punkt, spaCy) to detect sentence boundaries, then group sentences until you hit chunk size.

Why it matters. Naive splitting on the period character breaks "Dr. Smith arrived at 3 p.m. The talk lasted 45 min." into garbage. A real sentence tokenizer keeps the abbreviations, decimals, and ellipses intact.

Example. A legal contract: "Pursuant to Section 4.2, the Party shall remit payment to Acme Inc. within 30 days." Naive split breaks this into four pieces. NLTK Punkt keeps it whole.

Pros.

  • Handles abbreviations, decimals, ellipses without breaking mid-sentence.
  • Multilingual via spaCy models.
  • Cleaner inputs to downstream pipelines (sentence-window, propositions).

Cons.

  • Slower than character splitting (the sentence model has overhead).
  • Still document-blind. Ignores headings, tables, lists.
  • No help on code, JSON, HTML.

When to use. Prose-heavy corpora where abbreviation handling matters: legal text, narrative content, support tickets, transcripts.

When NOT to use. Code, structured data, or anywhere the structure carries more meaning than the prose.

Strategy 4: Document Structure-Aware Splitting

Workflow. Parse the document's native structure (Markdown headings, HTML tags, PDF layout, code AST) and split on the boundaries the author already wrote.

How it works. A MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter walks #, ##, ### and treats each section as a chunk. A PDF parser like Unstructured.io detects layout (titles, lists, tables, captions) and chunks by element type. A code splitter parses the AST and keeps functions and classes together.

Example. A pricing comparison table in a product doc stays as one chunk instead of being cut after row three. A Python class with five methods stays whole instead of being split mid-method. A ## Authentication section in an API doc stays self-contained, with its heading available as metadata for filtering.

Pros.

  • Tables stay intact - massive win for technical reference content.
  • Code functions and classes stay coherent.
  • Headings can be indexed as metadata for filtering and reranking.
  • Preserves what the author meant to be a unit.

Cons.

  • Requires a parser per format. PDF parsing is hard, OCR is harder.
  • Layout-rich PDFs need heavyweight tools (Unstructured.io hi-res, LlamaParse, Docling) which are slow and sometimes expensive.
  • Bad input markdown (no consistent heading levels) means bad chunks.

When to use. Always, as the bottom layer of your pipeline. Stack other strategies on top, never replace this floor. The single highest-ROI thing you can do for technical documentation, product manuals, regulatory filings, and code-heavy corpora.

When NOT to use. When your input is unstructured prose with no markup signal (raw transcripts, chat logs, OCR'd notes). Use sentence-based or recursive instead.

Strategy 5: Hierarchical (Parent-Child / Small-to-Big)

Workflow. Build a tree: document, then sections, then paragraphs. Embed the small leaves (paragraphs). At retrieval time, if multiple leaves under the same parent match the query, return the parent instead of the individual leaves. LlamaIndex calls this auto-merging.

How it works. You decouple the retrieval surface (small chunks, high precision) from the generation surface (larger chunks, full context). The retriever finds tight matches; the merger expands them upward when the matches cluster.

Example. API documentation. The query "how do I authenticate" matches three paragraphs under the ## Authentication section. Instead of returning three disjoint paragraphs, the retriever returns the whole section. The LLM gets coherent context; the user gets a coherent answer.

Pros.

  • Solves the precision-vs-context tradeoff explicitly.
  • Tight queries get tight chunks; broad queries get whole sections.
  • Decouples retrieval surface from generation surface, which is the right architectural call.

Cons.

  • More moving parts: tree storage, merge thresholds.
  • Storage roughly doubles or triples (you index leaves and store parents).
  • The merge threshold is a hyperparameter that needs evaluation.

When to use. Long documents with hierarchy: technical manuals, RFCs, regulatory filings, books, multi-section product docs. Anywhere the author already wrote a hierarchy and the query mix spans both narrow and broad questions.

When NOT to use. Short flat documents (tweets, FAQ entries, support tickets). There is no hierarchy to exploit and the added complexity buys you nothing.

Strategy 6: Semantic Chunking (the paper tiger)

Origin. Popularised by Greg Kamradt in his "5 Levels of Text Splitting" tutorial. Reimplemented as SemanticChunker in LangChain Experimental and SemanticSplitterNodeParser in LlamaIndex.

Workflow. Embed each sentence (or a sliding window of sentences). Walk the document; when adjacent-sentence cosine distance jumps above a threshold, insert a chunk boundary. The intuition is appealing: chunks should match topic boundaries, not character counts.

How it actually behaves in production.

  • Sentence embeddings are noisy on technical text. Vocabulary repeats across topics. The embedder sees the same words and reports high similarity even when the meaning has shifted. The signal it is supposed to fire on barely exists.
  • The threshold is a knob nobody tunes. Percentile, standard deviation, interquartile range - each has a different default and most teams ship the default. The default is wrong for any specific corpus.
  • You pay embedding cost at ingest with no guaranteed gain. Every sentence gets encoded, and the boundaries it picks are not provably better than the cheap separators recursive splitting already uses.
  • Independent evaluation pushes back hard. Chroma's 2024 chunking benchmark (linked at the bottom) ran the threshold-based semantic chunker against a recursive baseline and the recursive baseline was competitive or ahead on the metrics they reported. The marketing copy is stronger than the evidence. Their own clustering variant - covered next - did beat the baseline; pure threshold-walking did not.

Pros (theoretical).

  • Variable-sized chunks aligned to topic transitions.
  • No reliance on author markup.

Cons (in practice).

  • Loses to a far cheaper recursive baseline in independent evaluation.
  • Adds embedding compute at ingest with no consistent gain.
  • Brittle to threshold choice and embedder choice.

When to use. Honestly: only when you have measured a win on your specific corpus against a recursive baseline. The intuition is reasonable; the empirics are not on its side by default.

When NOT to use. As a default. The cost is real and the win is not consistent. If a vendor pitches it without showing you their eval, ask for the eval.

Strategy 7: Cluster Semantic Chunking

Workflow. Variant of semantic chunking that frames the problem as clustering instead of threshold-walking. Embed sentences, cluster them globally to maximise within-cluster similarity, treat the clusters as chunks. Proposed by Chroma in their 2024 chunking evaluation as their own novel method.

Why this beats the threshold-walking approach. Local pairwise similarity is fragile - one bad neighbour pair anywhere and the boundary is wrong. Clustering looks at the global structure of the document and is more robust to the noisy similarity signal that breaks the threshold approach.

Example. A research paper with related-work, methods, results, and discussion sections. Pairwise similarity may flicker as terminology recurs across sections. Clustering looks at the document holistically and groups sentences by their dominant theme.

Pros.

  • The semantic variant most worth trying, per Chroma's own evaluation.
  • More stable than threshold-walking.
  • Still no reliance on author markup.

Cons.

  • Still pays the embedding cost at ingest.
  • Reference implementation is research code, not yet a stable library API.
  • Gains are corpus-dependent. You still have to evaluate.

When to use. If you are committed to a semantic approach and want the variant with the best independent evidence behind it.

When NOT to use. Before you have beaten the recursive baseline on your own eval set. Fix that first. Cluster semantic is a refinement, not a foundation.

Strategy 8: Late Chunking

Workflow. Push the entire document through a long-context embedder first. Then split. Mean-pool the token vectors per chunk to get the chunk embedding. The split happens after the encode, not before.

How it works. In naive chunking, each chunk is embedded in isolation, blind to the rest of the document. With late chunking, every token vector has already attended to the whole document via the transformer's self-attention, so the chunk vectors carry context that the chunks themselves do not contain in their text.

Example. A long Kubernetes doc. A chunk reading "the controller watches for changes and reconciles state" is ambiguous on its own across Deployments, StatefulSets, and custom operators. With late chunking, the token vectors for that sentence already encode "this section is about StatefulSets" because they attended to the surrounding section. The chunk gets retrieved for the right resource, not the wrong one.

Pros.

  • Resolves cross-chunk references at the embedding level rather than the text level.
  • Gains track document length: the longer the document, the bigger the win.
  • Composes with any chunk-boundary algorithm above. You still pick where to split, you just embed differently.
  • The text content of the chunk is unchanged, so storage stays the same.

Cons.

  • Requires a long-context embedder. Many embedders still cap at 512 tokens; you need one that can ingest a whole document. Qualifying models include jina-embeddings-v2 (8K), nomic-embed-text-v1.5 (8K), OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large (8K), and voyage-3-large (32K).
  • Higher ingest compute - you encode whole documents instead of small chunks.
  • On short documents (FAQs, tweets, support emails), the gain shrinks toward zero. The technique cannot help if there is no surrounding context to encode.

When to use. Long continuous prose where chunks reference earlier context: legal contracts ("the Party" defined 30 paragraphs earlier), research papers, manuals, narrative books.

When NOT to use. Short documents. Or when your embedder is 512-token. Or when latency is your binding constraint and you cannot afford the heavier ingest pass.

Strategy 9: Contextual Retrieval

Workflow. Before embedding each chunk, run a cheap LLM pass that prepends a 50 to 100 token "where does this chunk sit in the document" header. Embed the chunk plus the header. Use prompt caching so the full document is cached and only the chunk varies per call - this is what makes the cost survivable.

How it works. The LLM gets the full document and the chunk, and answers a single question: "what context does this chunk need to be retrievable?" The answer is a short situating blurb. You concatenate that blurb to the chunk and embed the whole thing. Same chunk text, very different vector.

Example. A support KB chunk reads "set the timeout to 30 seconds in the advanced settings tab." On its own, useless. After contextualisation: "[Stripe Connect, API v2024-06-20, Webhook configuration] Set the timeout to 30 seconds in the advanced settings tab." Now the embedding encodes product, version, and section. Same chunk text, very different vector. The same chunk in a different product's KB gets a different prefix, and the two no longer collide in vector space.

Pros.

  • Strong externally validated retrieval gains in the current literature, particularly when combined with hybrid search (BM25 + dense) and a reranker on top. Anthropic reported large failure-rate reductions on their evaluation; results are corpus-dependent so confirm on yours.
  • Composable with every other strategy on this list - you can contextualise hierarchical chunks, structure-aware chunks, late-chunked vectors, all of them.
  • Reusable across embedders. The enrichment is text, not a vector tied to a specific model.

Cons.

  • One LLM call per chunk at ingest. Without prompt caching the bill is brutal; with it, manageable.
  • Adds tokens per chunk to your index storage.
  • Works best for static or slow-changing corpora. Rapidly changing corpora pay the LLM cost repeatedly.

When to use. Production RAG over heterogeneous corpora where the same phrase recurs with different meanings across documents (multi-product docs, multi-tenant data, multi-version APIs). When retrieval quality is your bottleneck and ingest cost is acceptable.

When NOT to use. Tiny knowledge bases. If your whole corpus fits in the model's context window, just put it all in the prompt with caching - simpler, cheaper, and the LLM does the retrieval implicitly.

How To Pick One

The defensible production stack is rarely a single strategy. It is a layer cake.

  1. Floor: document structure-aware splitting. Always.
  2. For long hierarchical docs: add hierarchical retrieval on top.
  3. For long continuous prose: consider late chunking on top.
  4. For heterogeneous corpora and serious retrieval quality: add contextual retrieval, hybrid BM25, and a reranker.

Recursive and sentence-based are starting points. Fixed-size is a baseline you compare against. Vanilla semantic chunking is a research curiosity that loses to cheaper alternatives. Cluster semantic is an experiment to run if you have an eval harness. Late chunking and contextual retrieval are the two strategies most likely to move the needle once the floor is in place.

What I Deliberately Left Off: Adaptive Chunking

There is a class of methods that do not pick a chunking strategy - they pick among strategies per document. The clearest recent example is Ekimetrics' Adaptive Chunking (github.com/ekimetrics/adaptive-chunking, accepted at LREC 2026), which scores candidate chunkings on five intrinsic quality metrics and selects the best one per document.

The intuition is right - no single chunker wins on every document. I am still leaving it off the production-recommendation list, because it has not had a year of independent production usage behind it yet. Revisit this list in a year.

The Only Benchmark That Matters

Every paper, blog, and benchmark you read was measured on someone else's corpus, with someone else's queries. None of them are yours. Build a golden set of 50 to 200 query-answer pairs from your real usage and measure each strategy against your retriever. Numbers from a slide deck have a half-life of weeks. Numbers from your own eval harness compound.

The chunker is upstream of everything. Get it right and the rest of your stack has a chance.

References

  • Anthropic: Introducing Contextual Retrieval
  • Jina AI: Late Chunking in Long-Context Embedding Models
  • Late Chunking paper (arXiv): https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.04701
  • Chroma Research: Evaluating Chunking Strategies for Retrieval
  • Pinecone: Chunking Strategies for LLM Applications
  • LangChain: Text Splitters API
  • LlamaIndex: Node Parsers / Splitters
  • Unstructured.io: Chunking
  • Chonkie: https://github.com/chonkie-inc/chonkie
  • Greg Kamradt: 5 Levels of Text Splitting

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud in the Age of AI and Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Overview :

SAP's latest innovations, announced at Sapphire 2026, mark a significant evolution in the role of ERP—from a system of record to a foundation for intelligent, AI-driven business operations. By integrating SAP Business Data Cloud, Business AI, and SAP Cloud ERP, SAP is enabling organizations to leverage trusted business data, gain deeper insights, and automate decision-making processes. For S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers, this evolution provides a pathway to accelerate innovation, improve operational efficiency, and unlock greater business value through data-driven and AI-powered capabilities.

Autonomous Enterprise Vision

SAP introduced the concept of the Autonomous Enterprise where AI agents can execute business processes across Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain, HR, and Sales with minimal human intervention.

SAP Business AI Platform

SAP unified:

  • SAP BTP
  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Business AI

into one governed AI platform

Business Data Cloud Becomes the Foundation

SAP clearly positioned BDC as the trusted data foundation for AI agents and Joule. BDC is no longer "just another analytics platform"; it is becoming the enterprise knowledge layer for SAP AI.

SAP HANA Cloud Inside BDC

A major announcement:

  • SAP HANA Cloud is now a core component of BDC.
  • Supports graph, vector, spatial, transactional and analytical workloads.
  • Enables AI reasoning directly on enterprise data.

Master Data Management in BDC

Through:

  • Reltio integration
  • SAP Master Data Governance integration

BDC becomes the place where trusted, governed master data lives.

Based on the above developments lot of benefits to our Cloud ERP customers.

Benefits For S/4HANA Public Cloud Customers

This is where the value becomes very tangible.

1. Faster Time to Insight

S/4HANA data is automatically available in BDC business data products.

Result:

  • Less extraction
  • Less replication
  • Faster analytics

2. Better AI Results

AI agents can understand:

  • Sales Orders
  • Purchase Orders
  • Suppliers
  • Customers
  • Financial Data

with business meaning, not just tables and fields.

3. Cross-Application Analytics

Customers can combine:

  • S/4HANA
  • SuccessFactors
  • Ariba
  • Concur
  • Non-SAP Data

inside one governed data layer.

For example use standatd Public Cloud data products as shown below and get value.

4. Reduced Data Silos

BDC acts as the common semantic layer across SAP applications.

5. AI-Driven Decision Making

Instead of:

"Show me overdue purchase orders."

Users will ask:

"Which suppliers are likely to delay deliveries next month and what actions should I take?"

Joule and AI agents will use BDC context to answer and act.

Key Takeaways : 

 

  • AI without business context has limited value.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud becomes the trusted business data foundation for AI.
  • SAP Business AI Platform combines AI, Data, and BTP into one architecture.
  • S/4HANA Public Cloud customers gain AI-ready business context, trusted data products, and autonomous business processes.

 

Thank you ! cheers ! Happy Learning !

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Supporting data migration from other systems (SAP Solution Manager, etc.)
entry.content:
Integration of several products is always a challenging task, data accuracy and consistency are paramount. With this April 30th release, we are taking a significant step forward in strengthening that.  

It is all about the process models. We now offer a mechanism to verify their uniqueness across the products which are integrated, like SAP Signavio, SAP Cloud ALM, SAP Solution Manager and others. Process models are created in SAP Signavio, then are sent to and reside in other systems as well. For example, you can model a process in SAP Signavio and send it to SAP Cloud ALM and to SAP Solution Manager or any other system. Then the same process model can be sent from SAP Solution Manager or any other application to SAP Cloud ALM.  

If an SAP Signavio process model exists in SAP Cloud ALM, when syncing it to SAP Cloud ALM, SAP Signavio will update it in SAP Cloud ALM. If SAP Signavio process model does not exist in SAP Cloud ALM, when syncing it to SAP Cloud ALM, SAP Signavio will create it in SAP Cloud ALM. 

We preserve the uniqueness of process models across products by introducing external reference, which is provided by every product and verified each time when there are attempts from SAP Signavio to create or update a process model in SAP Cloud ALM. SAP Cloud ALM stores the external reference of every process model like a single point of truth for cross-product reference. 

As a user you don’t have to do anything: the additional cross-product uniqueness verification is there for you. 

For further reference please find more information on the SAP Help Portal: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/signavio-process-transformation-suite/9552dd8c5c384ea1bf9f6e27fe0dcfe7/0c070733ff0140e7bd10b531bb6d6b21.html 

https://help.sap.com/docs/signavio-process-manager/user-guide/exporting-bpmn-diagrams-revisions-in-sap-signavio-to-sap-cloud-alm  (in the Note)

entry.title: Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized
entry.content:
Why SAP S/4HANA Transformations Fail When Business Processes Are Automated Before They Are Optimized

Introduction

One of the most common mistakes I have observed during SAP S/4HANA transformation programs is the tendency to automate existing business processes without first evaluating whether those processes are effective.

Organizations often invest significant time and resources implementing automation, workflows, robotic process automation (RPA), AI-powered assistants, and system integrations. However, if the underlying process is inefficient, inconsistent, or poorly designed, automation may simply accelerate existing problems rather than solve them.

Over the years, I have seen organizations achieve greater transformation success when they focus first on process optimization and then on automation.

The Automation Trap

Many transformation programs begin with the goal of reducing manual effort.

While this objective is understandable, teams sometimes assume that every manual activity should be automated.

Questions such as:

  • Can this approval be automated?

  • Can AI make this decision?

  • Can we eliminate manual intervention?

are frequently discussed.

However, a more important question is often overlooked:

Is this process designed correctly in the first place?

Automating an inefficient process rarely creates an efficient outcome.

When Automation Creates New Problems

In one transformation program, multiple approval layers had evolved over several years.

The process contained redundant approvals, overlapping responsibilities, and unnecessary delays.

Rather than simplifying the process, the organization initially focused on automating the approval workflow.

The result was predictable.

Approvals moved electronically instead of manually, but the overall cycle time remained largely unchanged because the process itself was still inefficient.

The organization eventually redesigned the approval structure, eliminated unnecessary steps, clarified ownership, and then automated the optimized workflow.

Only then did measurable improvements occur.

Process Optimization Before Automation

Before automation initiatives begin, organizations should evaluate:

Process Complexity

Does the process contain unnecessary steps?

Process Ownership

Is accountability clearly defined?

Business Value

Does each activity contribute meaningful value?

Standardization

Are different teams following the same process?

Compliance Requirements

Which controls are truly required?

Answering these questions often reveals opportunities to simplify operations before introducing automation.

The Role of SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA provides powerful capabilities for process standardization and automation.

However, the platform delivers the greatest value when organizations use the transformation as an opportunity to challenge existing practices.

Instead of asking:

"What should we automate?"

Organizations should first ask:

"What should we improve?"

The most successful transformations typically combine process redesign, organizational alignment, and technology modernization.

Where AI Fits Into the Equation

The growing adoption of AI introduces another layer of opportunity.

AI can:

  • Identify process bottlenecks

  • Detect inefficiencies

  • Predict delays

  • Recommend workflow improvements

  • Analyze user behavior patterns

However, AI is most effective when applied to well-defined and optimized business processes.

Organizations that deploy AI on top of inconsistent processes often struggle to achieve expected outcomes.

Signs That a Process Needs Optimization First

Several indicators suggest that optimization should occur before automation:

  • Frequent exceptions

  • Multiple approval layers

  • Duplicate data entry

  • Unclear ownership

  • High rework rates

  • Inconsistent execution across teams

  • Long cycle times

When these symptoms exist, process redesign should generally precede automation initiatives.

Lessons Learned

One lesson I have learned throughout SAP transformation programs is that technology is rarely the root cause of process inefficiency.

Most challenges originate from process design, governance, ownership, or organizational complexity.

Automation can amplify the effectiveness of a good process.

It can also amplify the inefficiencies of a poor one.

The difference lies in whether optimization occurs before automation.

Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA transformations provide organizations with a unique opportunity to rethink how work is performed.

Rather than simply digitizing existing processes, organizations should use transformation programs to simplify, standardize, and optimize operations before introducing automation.

In my experience, the most successful SAP programs are not those that automate the most processes. They are the ones that improve the right processes first and then apply automation where it creates meaningful business value.

Optimization before automation is often the difference between transformation and digitized inefficiency.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I'd add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money, and it's almost never a technical issue.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.
Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is "good enough" on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren't standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.
SMB owners assume the consultant will "handle it." What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can't process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup. Better model: identify 2-3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army.

3. No cutover rollback plan.
If something breaks on go-live night, what's the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you'd planned for it.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs
TekAI: AI-powered operations intelligence for SAP B1 users

entry.title: Re: Unable to Restrict Edit Functionality in Display Main...
entry.content:
Hello @sandeep_shankarampet,

I think the best starting point to figure out which catalog, IAM app, or authorization is granting Edit access is the app Display Authorization Trace.

  • Start a trace for the user with the affected role
  • Login as the user and open the app Display Maintenance Order
  • Then immediately deactivate the trace (to keep the amount of data in the trace manageable)
  • Then analyze the traces results with status "successful" and "filtered": since the edit access is granted, the relevant catalog, IAM app or restriction should be listed here.

If you would like to have some help with analysing the trace results, feel free to share them here.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Re: Asset Revaluation - SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello!

The posting record for revaluation of historical Acquisition and Production Costs (APC) debits the asset balance sheet account (APC) and credits a revaluation reserve account in equity. The accumulated depreciation adjustment is posted simultaneously debiting the revaluation adjustment account and crediting the accumulated depreciation account. You can set these postings using account determination groups.

For Portugal under SNC, this means account 5891 (Excedentes de Revalorização) should be configured directly as the credit account in Asset Accounting account determination for revaluation postings. There is no standard SAP clearing account step in between, the system posts straight to the equity account you configure.

Depreciation is automatically recalculated on the revalued asset base, surplus realisation to Retained Earnings is not automatic. After revaluation, the APC and depreciation have been adjusted accordingly; the planned depreciation amount for the remaining useful life is recalculated based on the new revalued carrying amount.

Under SNC, the portion of account 5891 that corresponds to the additional depreciation charged in each period should be progressively transferred to Retained Earnings (account 551x). SAP does not generate this transfer automatically. It must be posted manually via a periodic FI journal entry, typically at year-end, for the amount equal to: (depreciation based on revalued cost) minus (depreciation based on original cost).

Deferred taxes on asset revaluation are not automatically calculated or posted. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does include a Deferred Tax Processing framework (documented in the SAP Help Portal under Financial Closing), but this is designed for timing differences arising from revenue and expense recognition, not for deferred taxes arising from asset revaluation surplus under SNC. For the Portugal context, where a revaluation of a fixed asset creates a temporary difference between the tax base and the carrying amount the deferred tax liability must be posted manually.

There is no standard configuration in Public Edition that automates the calculation of revaluation-specific deferred taxes. SAP's deferred tax engine handles transactional temporary differences (e.g. provisions, accruals) but does not integrate with the Asset Accounting revaluation to post revaluation deferred tax automatically.

The recommended standard best practice for Portugal in Public Edition is:

  1.  Configure account determination to credit 5891 directly on revaluation posting. No clearing account needed.
  2.  Run revaluation via the Post Asset Revaluation app. The system posts the APC adjustment and accumulated depreciation offset in one document. From the next depreciation run, depreciation is automatically based on the revalued amount.
  3.  Year-end manual entries: Post two manual FI journal entries annually:

The progressive transfer from 5891 to Retained Earnings for the additional depreciation portion. And the partial reversal of the deferred tax liability corresponding to that year's realisation.

  1.  Deferred tax initial posting: At the time of revaluation, post the deferred tax liability manually (Dr 5891 ) for the full temporary difference. This reduces the net equity surplus shown in 5891 to its after-tax amount.

SAP References:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/00aed0aaf4a4423d99e71a1fb938e9c9.html?locale=en-US&q=Post+Asset+Revaluation

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/dc70b0b2798b4601a5c80bdbeca6e551.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=2602.500&q=Manual%20Revaluation%20in%20Asset%20Accounting

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3e5fcf2c768746049b5627bd5a42f720/a6a3c960b0b94d9aabc3570099c6398b.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=2602.500&q=Maintain%20Asset%20Revaluation%20Index

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/asset-accounting-asset-revaluation-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-2502/ba-p/14040197

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-members/asset-impairment-asset-revaluation-configuration-sap-s4hana/ba-p/13923367

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-finance-asset-accounting-frequently-asked/ba-p/13726519
